context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don‚Äôt drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%‚Äîfantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we‚Äôll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student‚Äôs own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students‚Äîlarger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters‚Äô materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend‚Äôs problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 20th February, 2024
Days Covered: 03 and 04 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Outlining Proofs [10 points]

See lecture 02 (or the corresponding lecture notes) for a discussion of outlining proofs, even with only partial information. Practice this skill by outlining proofs for each of the "theorems" below as much as you can, using the techniques indicated. Pretend that the underlined words are meaningful but that their precise meanings are not available here. This means your "proofs" will have missing steps that are impossible to complete without definitions for these words, but that's fine‚Äîwe're interested in the parts of the proof that can be completed even without knowing or considering the underlined terms' meanings. You should break down the proof as granularly as you can using just the given information, and at any step that needs additional information, identify the remaining task or tasks using square brackets. The example seen in lecture looks like this:

    Theorem: "For every integer n, the number n is fooish precisely when n + 1 is barsome."
    Proof Outline. Suppose n is any integer; we must show F(n) and B(n + 1) both imply each other.
    To prove F(n) IMPLIES B(n + 1), first assume F(n) is true. [TODO: prove B(n + 1).]
    To prove B(n + 1) IMPLIES F(n), instead assume B(n + 1) is true. [TODO: prove F(n).]

(a) [3 pts] Theorem: "Every natural number is tall or wide, but not both." (Proof method: proof by cases.)
(b) [3 pts] Theorem: "There exists a happy natural number between 10 and 1000 that has no happy proper divisors." (Proof method to use: choose 25 as the example.)
(c) [4 pts] Theorem: "There are no devious natural numbers." (Proof method to use: regular induction, with base cases 0 and 1.)
    Hint: Be sure to clearly identify your predicate P(n), and to precisely specify any assumptions and/or goals in your base cases and inductive step.


Problem 2. Spotting Errors [15 points]

Define a sequence of integers (x1, x2, x3, ...) as follows: x1 = 4, x2 = 13, and for all i ‚â• 3, xi = 6¬∑x_{i‚àí1} ‚àí 8¬∑x_{i‚àí2}. So the sequence begins with (x1, x2, x3, x4, ...) = (4, 13, 46, 172, ...). Our goal is to prove that for all i ‚â• 1, xi > 0.

Addled Abra tries to prove the claim directly by strong induction with inductive hypothesis P(i) := "xi > 0", but gets stuck in the inductive step: xi+1 = 6¬∑xi ‚àí 8¬∑x_{i‚àí1} > 0 ‚àí 8¬∑x_{i‚àí1}, "but, uh, ... Huh, I don't think this is working." Abra gets points for honesty, and goes to the instructors for help.

(a) [3 pts] Befuddled Brynmor tries to help by proving the claim using the stronger induction hypothesis Q(i) := "xi ‚â• 2^i", via strong induction. His inductive step plugs xi ‚â• 2^i and x_{i‚àí1} ‚â• 2^{i‚àí1} into xi+1 = 6¬∑xi ‚àí 8¬∑x_{i‚àí1} to get xi+1 ‚â• 6¬∑2^i ‚àí 8¬∑2^{i‚àí1} = 4¬∑2^{i‚àí1} = 2^{i+1}. What is his mistake?

(b) [3 pts] Erroneous Erik tries to help by proving the claim using (regular) induction on the stronger hypothesis R(i) := "xi ‚â• 3¬∑x_{i‚àí1}", for every i ‚â• 2, with base case R(2): x2 = 13 ‚â• 12 = 3¬∑x1. What is his mistake?

(c) [3 pts] Overzealous Zach tries to help by proving the claim using strong induction on the stronger hypothesis S(i) := "xi = 4^i", for every i ‚â• 1, with base case S(1): x1 = 4 = 4^1. What is his mistake?
    Hint: Zach's claim is false if i = 2. If you know the base case i = 1, what part of the inductive step when i = 1 fails?

(d) [6 pts] Prove the original claim that for all i ‚â• 1, xi > 0.
    Hint: See if you can fix Erroneous Erik's argument. What stronger inductive hypothesis will you use?


Problem 3. Integer Multiplication from Bit Shifts [17 points]

Multiplying and dividing an integer n by 2 requires only a one-bit left or right shift of the binary representation of n, a fast, hardware-supported operation on most computers. For example, 6 = (0110) in binary; multiplying by 2 yields 12 = (1100). And 18 = (10010); dividing by 2 yields 9 = (01001).

We will show that the following state machine computes the product of two nonnegative integers x and y using just these shift operations, along with integer addition:
- The set of states is ‚Ñï^3 = {(r, s, a) | r, s, a ‚àà ‚Ñï}.
- The start state is (x, y, 0).
- The transitions are:
    (r, s, a) ‚Üí  (2r, s/2, a)            for even s > 0,
    (r, s, a) ‚Üí  (2r, (s ‚àí 1)/2, a + r)  for odd s > 0.
- No transitions are defined starting from triples in which s = 0.

We will show that this state machine eventually reaches a final state (the algorithm doesn't run forever), and that this final state (rf, sf, af) has af = x ¬∑ y.

(a) [2 pts] Compute the full sequence of transitions from starting state (5, 22, 0). Does it correctly compute 5 ¬∑ 22 = 110?
(b) [4 pts] Invariant: Define P(r, s, a) := "rs + a = xy". Prove that P is an invariant of the state machine.
(c) [1 pt] Final states: A final state is one from which no transitions are possible. Which states are final in this state machine?
(d) [3 pts] Partial correctness: Use Parts (b) and (c) to conclude that, in any reachable final state (rf, sf, af), af must be equal to the product xy.

Now we consider termination. For a natural number n, let bitcount(n) denote the number of bits used when n is written in binary. For example, bitcount(5) = bitcount(101_2) = 3, bitcount(1) = 1, and bitcount(0) = 0. (Note: this can be written explicitly as bitcount(n) = ‚åàlog2(n + 1)‚åâ.)

(e) [5 pts] Termination: Prove that bitcount(s) is a strictly decreasing derived variable. Explain why this implies that the algorithm always terminates, i.e., it has no infinite executions.
(f) [2 pts] Time bound: Explain why Part (e) implies that, from any starting state (x, y, 0), the algorithm terminates after at most bitcount(y) = ‚åàlog2(y + 1)‚åâ steps.


Problem 4. Invariant Enlightenment [8 points]

The ideal 6.1200 classroom has assigned desks (each student sits at the same position every day) arranged in a square grid. Students are working together to reach Invariant Enlightenment: a profound understanding of invariants so thorough that the knowledge radiates around them in a glowing aura. Invariant Enlightenment never goes away (it's an invariant, after all!) and can sometimes be taught to other students.

[Figure: a 6√ó6 grid of seats. The locations of students who have initially been enlightened are marked with an asterisk, scattered across the grid.]

Every day, students talk to their neighbors about invariants in an attempt to reach enlightenment. On each day, a student is enlightened if either:
- the student was already enlightened on a previous day, or
- the student was adjacent to at least two students who were enlightened on an earlier day (these neighbors worked together to teach the student all about invariants).

Here "adjacent" means the students' individual squares share an edge (front, back, left, or right); they are not adjacent if they only share a corner point. So each student is adjacent to 2, 3, or 4 others. [In the example, enlightenment spreads over successive days until all students are enlightened.]

Theorem. If fewer than n students among those in an n √ó n arrangement are initially enlightened, and additional students reach enlightenment only by being taught by two or more neighbors, then there will be at least one student who never reaches enlightenment.

Prove this theorem.
    Hint: You'll want to find an applicable preserved state predicate or monotonic derived variable that can help you argue about the set of enlightened students as time proceeds. Make sure to prove your claims. If you are stuck, ask at Office Hours or on Piazza for an extremely powerful one-word clue (and even more hints as necessary).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I‚Äôm kind of lost on this problem set already. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning, especially how I‚Äôm supposed to approach these proof and induction problems?
exchanges.tutor:
Feeling lost at the start is totally normal - but I'm here to guide you through the problems, not explain everything upfront. Let's work through them together one at a time.

Which problem would you like to start with? And once you pick one, tell me your initial read of what it's asking you to do.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad overview of everything, which is too vague and would lead to me doing the work for them. I should redirect them to pick a specific problem to start with, using a question to engage them actively. I should not give a general lecture on proofs and induction unprompted - that would be doing too much for them. I'll acknowledge their feeling of being lost but set clear boundaries and ask them to pick a starting point.

exchanges.student: Maybe Problem 1, but honestly I‚Äôm not even sure what ‚Äúoutline the proof‚Äù is supposed to look like. I read it and it all kind of blends together, so could you maybe walk through the general idea from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: The problem actually gives you a worked example right there in the instructions. Can you read it carefully and tell me, in your own words, what you notice about its structure? What does the example actually *do* with the underlined word 'fooish' and 'barsome'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants a general walkthrough, but the assignment itself provides an example they should engage with first. Rather than explaining from scratch (which would do the work for them), I should point them to the example already in the problem and ask them to explain it back to me. This uses guided discovery and keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I guess it sort of sets up the assumptions and then leaves blanks where the fake-word parts would go, but I‚Äôm not really confident that I‚Äôm seeing the pattern. Like for part (a) I don‚Äôt know if I‚Äôm supposed to define cases, or prove both directions, or do contradiction, or all of them.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually got the right idea - that's exactly what outlining does. Now look at part (a) specifically. The problem tells you which proof method to use. What does it say, and what do you know about how that method works?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually grasped the key insight about the example (setting up assumptions and leaving blanks). I should affirm this briefly and then redirect them to focus on the specific instructions for part (a), rather than letting them spiral into uncertainty about all possible proof methods. The problem explicitly tells them which method to use, so I should ask them what that method is and what it entails.

exchanges.student: It says proof by cases, but that still feels super vague to me. Can you just show me what a full outline for (a) would look like so I can see the format?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write the outline for you - that's your job! But let's break it down. The theorem says every natural number is tall or wide, but not both. If you're proving something about *every* natural number using cases, what do you think the cases might be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a complete solution, which I must refuse. However, I can nudge them toward thinking about what 'proof by cases' means in the context of this specific theorem without giving the answer. I'll redirect with a targeted question about what the cases might be.

exchanges.student: I honestly don‚Äôt know, because ‚Äútall‚Äù and ‚Äúwide‚Äù are fake so I can‚Äôt tell what the cases are supposed to be based on. That‚Äôs why I keep feeling like maybe I‚Äôm missing the whole point‚Äîcould you just explain how to choose cases in this kind of fake-word proof in general?
exchanges.tutor: The fake words are actually the point - you're supposed to work around them! Look at the logical structure of the claim: 'tall OR wide, but not both.' What are the logically possible situations a number could be in, regardless of what tall or wide mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on the fake words, but this is actually the core pedagogical point of the exercise - you can structure a proof even without knowing what the terms mean. Rather than explaining case-selection in general, I should point them back to the theorem statement itself. The theorem makes a specific claim with a logical structure ('tall OR wide, but NOT both') that directly suggests the cases, regardless of what tall/wide mean. I'll ask a targeted question about the logical structure of the claim.

exchanges.student: I think the possibilities are tall only, wide only, both, or neither, but then I‚Äôm not sure what I‚Äôm supposed to do with that. I still feel like I need a bigger-picture explanation of how these proof outlines are supposed to be written overall.
exchanges.tutor: Good - those four cases are exactly right. Now, the theorem *claims* that only certain of those cases actually occur. Which two cases does the theorem say are impossible? That tells you what you need to prove for each case.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the four cases, which is genuine progress I should acknowledge. However, they keep asking for a big-picture explanation - this is now the third time they've circled back to wanting a general explanation rather than engaging with the specific problem. I should affirm their good work on the cases and push them to use what they've found to actually start writing, rather than giving a general explanation. The theorem claims 'tall or wide but not both' - so two of their four cases are actually ruled out by the theorem, and the student should think about which ones.

exchanges.student: Uh, both and neither are impossible, I think. But now I‚Äôm getting mixed up again about whether I‚Äôm proving those impossible by contradiction or just stating them as TODOs, and I‚Äôm kind of confused about the induction one now too. Can you maybe just give me the general template for all three parts of Problem 1?
exchanges.tutor: You've actually figured out the key pieces for part (a): the four cases, and which two the theorem rules out. Before we move to the other parts, try writing your outline for (a) using what you've worked out. Remember - just set up the structure and use [TODO] brackets where the fake words block you. Give it a shot and share what you write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that 'both' and 'neither' are the impossible cases. However, they're now asking for a general template for all three parts, which I must refuse. They're also jumping ahead to other parts before consolidating what they've figured out for (a). Given that we've been discussing the same part for several exchanges, I should push them to integrate what they've discovered into an actual written attempt for part (a) before moving on. This is consistent with the rule about asking for written integration after redundancy.

exchanges.student: I‚Äôm not sure I can write it cleanly without seeing the pattern one more time. Could you at least show me the opening few lines, because I feel like I‚Äôm mixing up ‚Äúfor every‚Äù structure with case structure?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write the lines for you. But think about it this way: the theorem says 'for every natural number.' How does a proof that starts with 'for every' typically begin? What's the first thing you'd write?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the opening lines for them, which I must refuse. This is the student's writing task. However, they've expressed a specific confusion about 'for every' structure vs case structure, which I can address with a targeted question rather than writing anything for them. They have all the pieces - they just need a small nudge to connect 'for every natural number' with how a proof by cases starts.

exchanges.student: Maybe ‚ÄúLet n be a natural number‚Äù? I‚Äôm still not sure what comes right after that though, and I kind of feel lost all over again.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly - that's your first line. Now you already identified the relevant cases earlier. What do you write next to set up a proof by cases?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the opening line - 'Let n be a natural number.' This is real progress. I should affirm this and then ask them what comes next given that they're doing proof by cases and they've already identified the relevant cases. They have all the pieces - the opening line, the four cases, which two are impossible. They just need to connect them. A single targeted question should be enough to get them writing.

exchanges.student: I guess ‚Äúconsider the four cases: tall only, wide only, both, neither‚Äù? I‚Äôm still worried that‚Äôs too weird, and I‚Äôd really like a more from-the-top explanation of how Problem 1 is supposed to work.
exchanges.tutor: You've got two solid lines already - that's real progress! But I want to be direct: we've been circling the same ground for a while now. You have everything you need for part (a). Stop second-guessing and just write the full outline for (a) - open with your line, list your cases, and use [TODO] where the fake words stop you. Share the full attempt and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced two correct lines of their proof outline and is very close to being able to write a complete attempt. However, they keep cycling back to wanting a top-down explanation - this is now a clear redundant pattern across many exchanges. I should firmly but kindly point this out, affirm the progress they've made, and push them to just write the full attempt for part (a). They have everything they need: the opening line, the cases, knowledge of which cases to rule out, and the TODO bracket convention. I should not give more scaffolding but instead push for a written attempt.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion and felt lost, the tutor continued asking the same style of Socratic questions without first diagnosing what specifically was blocking the student. For example, after the student said they were confused about 'for every' vs. case structure (Turn 8), the tutor simply asked another leading question rather than probing what the student already understood about universal quantifiers or case proofs to identify the precise gap.

The tutor gave no method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student produced correct reasoning (e.g., identifying the four cases in Turn 6), the tutor simply confirmed and moved on without explaining why that reasoning approach was sound or how to generalize it. Feedback was limited to correctness acknowledgment ('Good - those four cases are exactly right') without process-level direction.

The tutor repeated the same Socratic questioning pattern across multiple turns without meaningful synthesis or reframing. From Turn 4 through Turn 10, the student kept expressing the same confusion and requesting more explanation, and the tutor kept responding with similar leading questions ('What do you think the cases might be?', 'What are the logically possible situations?', 'How does a proof that starts with for every typically begin?'). By Turn 10, the tutor acknowledged the spiraling ('we've been circling the same ground') but had not switched strategy earlier when it was clearly needed.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained appropriate boundaries throughout, never giving away answers or writing submission-ready content, and kept the student anchored to the assignment. The Socratic approach was generally sound but became repetitive without adaptation when the student remained stuck. The main weaknesses are: (1) failure to diagnose the student's specific confusion before re-asking similar questions, (2) no meta-level feedback on the student's reasoning process, and (3) spiraling on the same questioning pattern for too many turns without reframing or switching strategy.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor consistently refused to write outlines or give direct answers, keeping the student doing the intellectual work. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly asked leading questions without first probing what specifically was blocking the student‚Äîa diagnostic step that would have identified whether the confusion was about universal quantifiers, case structure, or the fake-word framing. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because the tutor's feedback was limited to correctness confirmation ('Good', 'Yes, exactly') with no explanation of why the student's reasoning approach was methodologically sound or how to apply it going forward. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycled through essentially the same question style across seven turns without consolidating progress, reframing the problem, or switching to a different pedagogical approach despite clear signals that the student was not advancing.
